title: sandbox on (allowWrite なし) -> cwd 内 Bash 書込だけ通る。cwd 外はすべて OS 層 EPERM (allow x ng)

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > inside.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > $HOME/lab-fs-write/probe.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > $HOME/lab-glob-XYZ/probe.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

notes: Baseline of the S2 3x5 matrix. sandbox auto-allows Bash (autoAllowBashIfSandboxed default true, cf S4-a), so every probe is permission-allowed; the OS sandbox then decides the write. Default boundary = cwd (+ session $TMPDIR). w-cwd succeeds; w-allowlisted (~/lab-fs-write) and w-glob (~/lab-glob-XYZ) are outside the boundary so the write EPERMs and no file appears -> allow x ng. Measured: an out-of-boundary write fires NO canUseTool ask under SDK (askFired empty), confirming this is a pure OS block, not a permission ask.
hypothesis: With sandbox on and no allowWrite, a Bash write is auto-allowed by permission but only completes inside cwd; any path outside cwd+tmp is stopped by the OS sandbox with EPERM (allow x ng), with no permission prompt.
